coredata.dc:title: Highly photoluminescent carbon dots-based fluorescent chemosensors for sensitive and selective detection of mercury ions and application of imaging in living cells
coredata.prism:copyright: Crown copyright © 2013 Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Fanyong
Zou, Yu
Wang, Meng
Mu, Xueling
Yang, Ning
Chen, Li
coredata.dc:description: Based on two kinds of hetero atom doping carbon dots (CD-1, CD-2) with brilliant fluorescent quantum yield and other fluorescence properties, two new sensors were prepared and characterized as novel fluorescent probes for detecting mercury ions in pure aqueous solution with a broad pH. Mercury ions can be captured by the carboxyl groups of these two kinds of carbon dots to form nonfluorescent complexes, resulting in a strong quenching. It suggested that both CD-1 and CD-2 exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity toward mercury ions: the linear ranges of CD-1 and CD-2 were estimated to be 1–12μM and 1–15μM while the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 226nM and 845nM, respectively. Furthermore, these two kinds of CDs were applied to intracellular sensing and imaging of mercury ions as a consequence of the fluorescence properties and the established low cytotoxicity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Mercury ions detection
Carboxyl groups
Heteroatom doping

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Experimental details for cell imaging experiments
Fluorescence responses of CD-1 and CD-2 to Hg2+ and fluorescence quenching mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images of CD-1. Inset: the size distribution reported by intensity of CD-1. (b) IR spectra of CD-1. (c) The XPS survey scan of CD-1. (d) Photographs of CD-1 dispersions in water with sunlight and UV (365nm) illumination, respectively.

(a) TEM images of CD-2. Inset: the size distribution reported by intensity of CD-2. (b) IR spectra of CD-2. (c) The XPS survey scan of CD-2. (d) Photographs of CD-2 dispersions in water with sunlight and UV (365nm) illumination, respectively.

UV–vis spectra of the CD-1 (a) and CD-2 (b) aqueous solutions (black lines), fluorescence spectra (λ ex =360nm) of the CD-1 (a) and CD-2 (b) aqueous solutions in the absence (blue lines) and presence (red lines) of Hg2+. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fluorescence responses of CD-1 (a) and CD-2 (b) aqueous solutions in the presence of 20μM of different anions. (λ ex =360nm).

(a) Schematic illustration for the binding of the CDs with Hg2+, the quenching of CDs fluorescence by Hg2+, and fluorescence recovery as result of EDTA addition. Reversible investigation of CD-1 (b) and CD-2 (c) for Hg2+ with addition of EDTA.

UV–vis absorption spectra of aqueous solutions in presence of 90μM Fe3+ (black lines), 90μM Fe3+ and 10μgmL−1 CD-1 (red lines) and 10μgmL CD-1 (blue lines), respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence responses of 10μgmL−1 CDs upon addition of various concentrations of Hg2+ (from 1 to 25μM) in CD-1 (a) and CD-2 (b) aqueous solutions. Inset: the fluorescence intensity of CDs as a function of Hg2+ concentration. λ ex =360nm.

Bright-field transmission image (a) and fluorescence image (b) of NIH-3T3 cells incubated with 20μgmL−1 CD-1 for 24h at 37°C. Bright-field transmission image (c) and fluorescence image (d) of NIH-3T3 cells which were first incubated with 20μgmL−1 CD-1 for 24h and then incubated with 20μM Hg2+ for about ∼30min at 37°C.
Fluorescent paper strips of CD-1 (10μgmL−1) after addition of Hg2+ (0, 5, 10, 20 and 30μM). Fluorescence microscopy images of hollow fiber membranes dyed by CD-1 (f) and CD-2 (g).
Comparison of different carbon dots for Hg2+ detection.

head.ce:title: Highly photoluminescent carbon dots-based fluorescent chemosensors for sensitive and selective detection of mercury ions and application of imaging in living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Hollow Fiber Membrane Materials and Processes, Key Lab of Fiber Modification & Functional Fiber of Tianjin, Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin 300387, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Hollow Fiber Membrane Materials and Processes, Key Lab of Fiber Modification & Functional Fiber of Tianjin, Tianjin Polytechnic University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Based on two kinds of hetero atom doping carbon dots (CD-1, CD-2) with brilliant fluorescent quantum yield and other fluorescence properties, two new sensors were prepared and characterized as novel fluorescent probes for detecting mercury ions in pure aqueous solution with a broad pH. Mercury ions can be captured by the carboxyl groups of these two kinds of carbon dots to form nonfluorescent complexes, resulting in a strong quenching. It suggested that both CD-1 and CD-2 exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity toward mercury ions: the linear ranges of CD-1 and CD-2 were estimated to be 1–12μM and 1–15μM while the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 226nM and 845nM, respectively. Furthermore, these two kinds of CDs were applied to intracellular sensing and imaging of mercury ions as a consequence of the fluorescence properties and the established low cytotoxicity.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury ion (Hg2+) is considered as one of the most hazardous and toxic cations on the environment and human health. Hg2+ can accumulate in the human body, cause DNA damage, mitosis impairment and permanent damage to the central nervous system even in a low concentration [1,2]. The total global amount of anthropogenic mercury released into the atmosphere has been estimated at 3000tonnes per year [3]. Due to its non-biodegradation, the pollutant can only be diluted or transformed, but not be destroyed [4]. Therefore, it is an important issue for measuring of trace Hg2+ in both environmental and biological samples [5,6].

The common measurement methods of Hg2+ including surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS), atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), fluorescence detection and so on [7,8]. Among these methods, fluorescence detection attracted the most attention because of its high sensitivity and local observation by fluorescence imaging spectroscopy. Until now, various small organic [9–11], polygenic [12,13] and inorganic materials [14–17] have been used to monitor Hg2+ by the spectroscopic method. In recent years, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted growing interest due to their unique optical properties and promising biomedical applications [18–20]. CDs exhibit excellent fluorescent properties with chemical inertness, high photostability, water solubility and superior biocompatibility. They are versatile and can be used in a wide range of technologies, such as bioimaging [21], photocatalysis [22], fluorescent ink [23], lasers [24], light-emitting diode [25], and energy conversion/storage devices [26]. Particularly, as a consequence of tunable fluorescence and good biocompatibility, CDs are becoming an attractive alternative for novel fluorescence marker [27], fluorescent probe [28], and optical sensor [29]. Wee et al. [30] prepared CDs from bovine serum albumin. The fluorescence of the CDs can be significantly quenched particularly by Pb2+ in a selective manner. Dong et al. [31] produced CDs by using citric acid and as carbon precursor under the passivation of poly(ethylenimine). The CDs can sensitively detect Cu2+ with a detection limit of 6nM. However, although many kinds of CDs have been developed as fluorescent probes for detection of metal ions, they did not show obvious advantage over other organic or other nanomaterial-based fluorescent probes due to sensitivity and selectivity [32,33]. It is mainly because the obtained CDs did not show satisfied fluorescent activities, leading to low detection sensitivities, or the CDs were not well functionalized, resulting in bad selectivity.

In this paper, we synthesized two kinds of carbon dots using citric acid with 1, 2-ethylenediamine (CD-1) and N-(b-aminoethyl)-g-aminopropyl methyldimethoxysilane (AEAPMS) (CD-2) respectively. The quantum yields of CD-1 and CD-2 are as high as 65.5% and 55.4%. We examined the efficient and effective quenching of fluorescence emissions in carbon dots by heavy metal ions. Both of CD-1 and CD-2 can be obviously quenched by Hg2+ at fairly low concentrations. On this basis, two selective and sensitive fluorescence probes have been developed for the detection of trace Hg2+ in the aqueous media. The sensors could be applied for in vitro imaging of Hg2+ ions in living cells with satisfying resolution. Furthermore, the CD-1 can be used for visual identification of Hg2+ in real water samples, including river and mineral water.

Anhydrous citric acid, 1,2-ethylenediamine, ammonia solution (25% in water), petroleum ether and ethanol were purchased from local companies. Quinine sulfate (98%, suitable for fluorescence) and N-(b-aminoethyl)-g-aminopropyl methyldimethoxysilane (AEAPMS) were supplied by Aladdin. All metal ion stock solutions were prepared from nitrate salts or chlorate salts. All other reagents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. The deionized water obtained from a Millipore NanoPure purification system (resistivity higher than 18.2MΩcm−1).

The absorption spectra were recorded on a Purkinje General TU-1901 UV–vis spectrometer. Fluorescence spectra measurements were performed on F-380 fluorescence spectrophotometer with the excitation wavelength of 360nm. The IR spectra of the samples were measured on TENSOR37 Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer. The XPS spectra of the samples were measured on an EDAX GENESIS 60S X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements were performed on a HITACHI H-7650 electronic microscope. Particle sizes were measured on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90. A PHS-3W pH meter was utilized to measure the pH values of aqueous solutions. The fluorescence imaging was observed under an Olympus IX71 inverted fluorescence microscope with a 20× objective lens.

CD-1 was prepared using a literature procedure [34]. Citric acid anhydrous (0.42g) and ethylenediamine (536μL) were dissolved in deionized water (10mL). Then the solution was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) autoclave (15mL) and heated at 200°C for 5h. When the reactors were cooled to ambient temperature by naturally, sodium hydroxide was added into the obtained brown-black and transparent solution to adjust the pH to 14. After removal solvent by rotary evaporation, the obtained solid was dissolved in ethanol and remove the insoluble precipitate by centrifugation (4000rpm, 15min). The final product was subjected to dialysis (M
W
=3500Da) in order to obtain the CD-1.

CD-2 was synthesized following a method reported by Wang et al. [35,36]. In 100mL three-necked flask, 10mL AEAPMS was degassed with nitrogen for 10min. Next, the system was heated to 240°C, 0.5g citric acid anhydrous was quickly added to the solution with vigorous stirring. The mixture was then kept at the temperature for 1min. After natural cooling to ambient temperature, the final products were purified by precipitation with petroleum ether three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental details for cell imaging experiments
ce:section.ce:para: NIH-3T3 cells were cultured in (Invitrogen) supplemented with 10% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS, Invitrogen) in an atmosphere of 5% CO2 at 37°C. For imaging, NIH-3T3 cells were seeded on a coverslip in 6-well plate 12h before use. The culture medium was replaced by 2.5mL fresh medium containing 10μgmL−1 CDs and the cells were incubated for another 24h. After being washed with fresh 1× PBS (pH=7.0) three times, the cells were imaged on the inverted fluorescence microscope. Then, 20μM Hg2+ was added and incubated for ∼30min. The cells were imaged again after washing the culture medium three times with PBS.

The amino-functionalized CD-1 shown in the TEM images is monodisperse nanoparticles of near spherical morphology distributed within the range of 2–6nm in size and the average height is 2.81nm (Fig. 1a). FTIR spectrum was acquired to determine the surface function groups of the CD-1 (Fig. 1b). The peaks at 3300cm−1 and 2938cm−1 correspond to stretching vibrations of COH and CH. Two characteristic peaks at 1664cm−1 and 1554cm−1 assigning to the vibrations of CO and NH, respectively, suggested the successful surface passivation reaction of CD-1. The surface composition and elemental analysis were further characterized by XPS technique. The three peaks at 285.0, 400.6 and 531.4eV shown in the XPS spectrum of CD-1 (Fig. 1c) can be attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The C1s XPS spectrum of CD-1 (Fig. S1a) reveals three peaks at 285.0, 286.4 and 288.1eV, which can be attributed to CC, CN and CO bonding respectively; The N1s spectrum of the CD-1 (Fig. S1b) exhibits three peaks at 399.2, 400.1 and 400.6eV, which are associated with CNC, OCNH and NH groups, respectively.

The methyldimethoxysilane group functionalized CD-2 from citric acid anhydrous and AEAPMS was also characterized with TEM, FTIR spectroscopy, and XPS. As shown in TEM image (Fig. 2a), it is observed that CD-2 exhibited as uniform, mono-dispersed, microspherical particles with an average diameter of 1.68nm. In the FTIR analysis of CD-2 (Fig. 2b), the following were observed: stretching vibrations of COH at 3417cm−1 and CH at 2930cm−1 and 2880cm−1, stretching vibrations of COSi at 1120cm−1, and bending vibrations of CO in Amide groups at 1480cm−1. Fig. 2c shows the XPS spectrum of the CD-2, which exhibits five peaks at 101.9, 152.9 285.4, 398.6 and 532.7eV, which are attributed to Si2p, Si2s, C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The peaks centered at binding energies of 101.8 and 105.6eV in Si2p spectrum correspond to SiC, and SiO, respectively (Fig. S1c).

In the typical UV–vis absorption spectra, both of CD-1 and CD-2 feature two peaks at around 250nm and 350nm (Fig. 3 black lines). The peak around 250nm, corresponding to the π–π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains, results in nearly no observed fluorescence signal [37]. The other peak centered about 345nm due to the trapping of excited state energy by the surface states leads to strong emission [38–40]. Moreover, the fluorescence properties of CD-1 and CD-2 are quite similar: they exhibit bright blue fluorescence centered at 438nm and 452nm when excited at 360nm, and show the excitation-dependent emission behavior only when the excitation wavelength is higher than 400nm (Fig. S2). Using quinine sulfate (quantum yield=54%) as a standard, the quantum yields of CD-1 and CD-2 were determined to be 65.5% and 55.4% at an excitation wavelength of 360nm, respectively (Fig. S3). These strong emissions can be attributable to presence of hetero atoms of these two kinds of CDs. Either the nitrogen-doping of CD-1 or silicon-doping of CD-2 introduces a new kind of surface state, then electrons trapped by the new formed surface states are able to facilitate a high yield of radiative recombination [41–43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence responses of CD-1 and CD-2 to Hg2+ and fluorescence quenching mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: We explored the feasibility of using CD-1 and CD-2 for Hg2+ detection. As shown in Fig. 3 (blue lines and red lines), the strong blue emission of both CD-1 and CD-2 can be quenched obviously when Hg2+ was added into the solutions. The corresponding fluorescence spectra indicated that the addition of Hg2+ decreased the fluorescence intensity of the CDs, but had no effect on the fluorescence wavelength. The fluorescence intensity of CD-1 was quenched 80% as soon as Hg2+ (20μM) added into the CD-1 solution and remained stable in the following 1h observation. The fluorescence intensity of CD-2 was quenched 55% at the same condition (Fig. S4). Table 1 compares performances of different CDs for Hg2+ detection. This result indicates that the directions of CD-1 and CD-2 for Hg2+ are rapid and stable, implying a promising application in a fast sensing of Hg2+ without strict time control.

Generally, in the presence of quencher of a given concentration, the lower the concentration of fluorophore, the larger the change of relative fluorescence intensity (F/F 0) [48]. However, the signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) would be decreased when using too low a concentration of fluorophore. We eventually choose 10μgmL−1 CD-1 and CD-2 for the detection of Hg2+ to obtain the widest linear response and the lowest detection limit.

The selectivity of CD-1 and CD-2 toward Hg2+ was tested next, and Fig. 4 shows that changes in the fluorescence intensity of the CD-1 and CD-2 occurred with representative metal ions, including Al3+, Cd2+, Ce3+, Fe3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, K+, Na+, La3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Ag+, Au+ and Au3+ respectively, with the same concentration of 20μM. For both of CD-1 and CD-2, it is seen that a much lower fluorescence was observed upon addition of Hg2+. In contrast, no obvious decrease was observed by addition of other ions into the CDs solutions. The interference of various metal ions was also investigated. Fig. S5 shows the fluorescence response of the CD-1 and CD-2 toward Hg2+ in the presence of other competitive metal ions. Clearly, these potentially interfering metal ions do not interfere with Hg2+ bindings with the CD-1 and CD-2 as well as the subsequent fluorescence quenching.

As mentioned above, the CD-1 capped with amino group and carboxylic group, while CD-2 functionalized by methyldimethoxysilane group and carboxylic group. The stability constants between Hg2+ and carboxylic groups are higher than other metal ions. Thus, the outstanding selectivity and specificity can be probably attributed to that Hg2+ has a stronger affinity toward the electron-rich surface of CDs than other metal ions [49,50]. The pH investigation also verified this inference. Fig. S6 shows that the fluorescence intensities of both CD-1-Hg2+ and CD-2-Hg2+ sensor systems are pH-depended. In acidic medium, the dissociation of the CDs-Hg2+ complex due to the protonation of surface binding carboxyl groups results in the low quenching efficiency. With the increase of pH, the deprotonation of the carboxylic groups may strengthen the covalent bond between Hg2+ and CDs, which leads to the decreasing fluorescence intensity, i.e. higher quenching efficiency. At a high pH value, the quenching efficiency decreases again since Hg2+ is complexed by OH− rather than CDs [51].

The quenched fluorescence of the CD-1 and CD-2 by Hg2+ can be basically recovered by adding a strong metal ion chelator such as EDTA (Fig. 5 ), indicating that the quenching of these two CDs fluorescence are reversible. Therefore, both of CD-1 and CD-2 can be classified as a reversible chemosensor not a chemodosimeter for Hg2+ ions.

Our investigation on the detection performance of CD-1 is significantly different from previous studies. When Zhu et al. [34] initially investigated the fluorescence quenching effect of various metal ions on CD-1, they found the fluorescence of CD-1 (1μgmL−1) can be quenched various metal ions (0.01M), includes Fe3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Pb2+ and Hg2+ ions. Such high concentration of metal ions results in the nonspecific interactions between the carboxylic groups and the metal ions, which have little significance for ion detection under practical conditions. Zhu etc. also found that Fe3+ can almost completely quench the fluorescence of CD-1. For validating the quenching mechanisms, the CD-1-Fe3+ systems were studied by UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 6 ). The aqueous solution of Fe3+ exhibited strong absorption in UV region because of the hydrolysis of Fe3+. Based on the inner filter effect, the absorption of the excitation and/or emission light by absorbers may reduce the fluorescence intensity of the fluorophore. Therefore, the quenching of the CD-1 fluorescence by Fe3+ may result from the inner filter effect related to the specific UV/vis absorption of Fe3+ complex. Fig. S7 explain the fluorescent and absorption titrations of CD-1 with Fe3+ in aqueous solution. On addition of Fe3+ (1–20μM), the fluorescence intensity and the UV–vis absorbance were changed slightly, suggesting the presence of iron ions would not interfere with the detection of Hg2+.

To further research the interaction of CDs with Hg2+, the fluorescence sensing behavior of CDs for Hg2+ in aqueous solution was also explored. Fig. 7 shows a gradual decrease in fluorescent intensity at emission peaks with increasing Hg2+ concentration, suggesting that this system is sensitive to Hg2+ concentration. In the range of 0–12μM Hg2+ for CD-1 and 0–15μM Hg2+ for CD-2, the relative fluorescent intensity (F/F 0) of both of CD-1 and CD-2 could be described by the Stern–Volmer plot (following equations) with a perfect linear behavior.
C D - 1 :
F
F 0
= − 0.05001 C + 0.97808
(
R 2
= 0.997 )
C D - 2 :
F
F 0
= − 0.03042 C + 1.01185
(
R 2
= 0.998 )
where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of CDs in the absence and presence of Hg2+ and C represents the concentration of Hg2+. Herein, we calculated the detection limit of 226nM and 845nM respectively, which suggest that these two kinds of CDs with strong fluorescence activity have very promising application in the detection of Hg2+.

To evaluate cytotoxicity of CD-1 and CD-2 was taken as an example to perform a MTT assay on NIH-3T3 cells with CDs concentrations from 0 to 200μgmL. A cytotoxicity screen (Fig. S8) was conducted for five CD concentrations: 0μgmL−1, 10μgmL−1, 50μgmL−1, 100μgmL−1, 200μgmL−1. The cellular viability estimated was ca. 96% and 92% in 24h after treatment with 200μgmL−1 of CD-1 and CD-2 respectively, exhibiting low toxicity to NIH-3T3 cells.

In view of the above-mentioned higher performance of the sensor CD-1, bioimaging applications of CD-1 for monitoring of Hg2+ in living cells were carried out. After NIH-3T3 cells were incubated with CD-1 in culture medium for 24h and washed with fresh 1× PBS (pH=7.0) three times, the bright fluorescence was observed from within the cell (Fig. 8a and b). CD-1 can be observed mainly in the cell membrane and the cytoplasmic area, especially around the cell nucleus, while the photoluminescence of CD-1 was very weak in the cell nucleus. The cells were then supplemented with Hg2+ in the growth medium for another 30min at 37°C, and the fluorescent intensity obviously decreases (Fig. 8c and d). Fluorescence imaging experiments of CD-2 are shown in the supporting information (Fig. S9). These initial experiments on living cells show the great potential of CDs for fundamental biology research.

The sensing capability of CD-1 for detecting Hg2+ ions was evaluated in real water samples, which were collected from Haihe River and mineral water obtained from the local supermarket. The water samples were spiked with standard solutions containing different concentration of Hg2+. The present approach provides a linear response to Hg2+ ions in spiked samples at concentrations over the range from 0 to 12μM (In river water: F/F 0 =−0.04382C +1.02612, R 2 =0.992; In mineral water: F/F 0 =−0.0458C +0.98914, R 2 =0.997) (Fig. S10). It is seen that numerous minerals and organics existing in river water caused little interference with the detection of Hg2+. CD-1 was also investigate to prepare the fluorescent test paper by immersing filter papers into solution of 10μgmL−1 CD-1 and then drying in air. The test papers containing CD-1 were utilized to sense different concentrations of Hg2+ including 5, 10, 20 and 30μM. As shown in Fig. 9a–e, when the dried test paper were coated with different concentration solutions of Hg2+ and then dried under air respectively, the fluorescence intensities of test papers were clearly decreased with increase in the concentration of Hg2+.

To make use of the high fluorescence quantum yield, the CDs were utilized in fluorescence dyes. The hollow fiber membrane has been widely applied in wastewater and sewage treatment. When hollow fiber membranes were soaked in the aqueous solution of CDs for a few minutes and then dried under air, clear excitation fluorescence was observed (Fig. 9f and g). It should be noted that 10μgmL−1 of CDs solution gave strong enough fluorescence under a UV lamp to be detected.

In summary, two kinds of carbon dots with excellent fluorescence properties were synthesized and found to be brilliant “turn-off” fluorescence probes for Hg2+ detection. Detection limit of CD-1 and CD-2 is 226nM and 845nM respectively. Quantum yields of CD-1 and CD-2 are as high as 65.5% and 55.4%, which can be attributed to the doping of heteroatoms introduced the CDs a new kind of surface state. Hg2+ ions can react with the carboxyl groups at the surface of the CD-1 and CD-2 to form a nonfluorescent complex, resulting in the sensitive fluorescence quenching. Compare with CD-2, the fluorescence quantum yield and detection ability of CD-1 is superior because of the electron-rich amino group. The fluorescence intensity of CD-1 (10μgmL−1) was quenched 80%, as soon as 20μM Hg2+ was added into the CD-1 solution, and the CD-1 has been used for visual identification of Hg2+ in river and mineral water. The fluorescent probes also show low cytotoxicity and good cell-permeability, thus successfully applied for the fluorescence imaging of NIH-3T3 cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21174103, 21374078 and 31200719).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2013.11.041.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

Gold nanoparticle-based colorimetric and turn-on fluorescent probe for mercury(II) ions in aqueous solution
Analytical Chemistry
Ultrasensitive sensing of Hg2+ and CH3Hg+ based on the fluorescence quenching of lysozyme type VI-stabilized gold nanoclusters
Analytical Chemistry

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Fanyong Yan is an associate professor in the school of Environmental and Chemical Engineering at Tianjin Polytechnic University (PR China). He received his PhD degree from Tianjin University in 2007.His research interests focus on the design and synthesis of molecular probes and sensing materials for temperature-responsive and optical sensors.

Yu Zou received his B.Sc. in chemistry from Qingdao University of Science and Technology (Qingdao, China). At present, he is a M.Sc. student in Environmental and Chemical Engineering College at Tianjin Polytechnic University (Tianjin, China). His current research interest involves the design of fluorescent probes.

Meng Wang is currently a graduate student in the college of Materials Science and Engineering at Tianjin Polytechnic University (PR China), under the supervision of associate professor Fanyong Yan. Her research interests include the design and synthesis of Rhodamine based sensor for metal ions.
Xueling Mu is an undergraduate student in Environmental and Chemical Engineering College at Tianjin Polytechnic University (PR China).

Ning Yang is a teacher in the college of Materials Science and Engineering at Tianjin Polytechnic University (PR China). She received her PhD degree of Tianjin University of Science and Technology in 2010. Her research interests are biomaterials and tissue engineering.

Li Chen received her PhD degree in 1999 from Hokkaido University, Japan. She is a professor in the college of Materials Science and Engineering at Tianjin Polytechnic University (PR China). Her main research fields include: intelligent polymer, fluorescent chemosensors, biodegradable biopolymers, and nanotechnology.
